relations.note: Example data-tone sentence uses the DM abbreviation after first mention.

quotes: every standalone section must establish `dermatomyositis (DM)` at its own first mention
After first mention of "dermatomyositis," use "DM" in running copy to avoid repetition.
Do NOT use "DM" in headlines, headings, or subheads
[GENERAL] every standalone section must establish `dermatomyositis (DM)` at its own first mention

rules.intent: Ensure clarity, accessibility, and SEO while allowing concise running copy across assembled sections.
rules.rule_text: After the first mention of "dermatomyositis" (pattern: `dermatomyositis (DM)`), use "DM" in running copy to avoid repetition, but NEVER use "DM" in headlines, headings, or subheads — always the full word there (clarity, accessibility, SEO). Because sections are assembled later, every standalone section must establish `dermatomyositis (DM)` at its own first mention unless the assembly brief states the abbreviation is established upstream.

rules.intent: Maintain inclusive, person-first language for patient audiences.
rules.rule_text: In patient materials, say "someone with dermatomyositis" / "people with dermatomyositis" — not "patients" ("patients" is acceptable only in HCP materials). Use "care team" (not "staff") for the network of patients, HCPs, and support systems.

quotes: Exceptions — always numerals: percentages, ages, ratios.

rules.rule_text: Spell out zero–nine; use numerals for 10+. Always use numerals for percentages, ages, and ratios. Percentages use the % sign (e.g., 74%).

quotes: Oxford/serial comma always in series of 3+.

rules.rule_text: Always use the Oxford/serial comma in series of 3+.

quotes: when citing clinical data, avoid negative or fear-based language; use an empathetic, supportive, trust-building tone without oversimplifying.

rules.intent: Keep clinical-data messaging empathetic and trust-building.
rules.rule_text: When citing clinical data, avoid negative or fear-based language; use an empathetic, supportive, trust-building tone without oversimplifying. Example pattern: "Participants in the VALOR study taking LISRAYA had nearly 50% more improvement in their DM symptoms after one year compared to placebo."
